name: inverse leg curl (on pull-up cable machine)
instructions: Adjust the cable machine so that the ankle straps are at the lowest setting.
Lie face down on the bench with your legs extended and the ankle straps attached to your feet.
Hold onto the handles of the bench for stability.
Bend your knees and curl your legs towards your glutes, squeezing your hamstrings.
Pause for a moment at the top of the movement, then slowly lower your legs back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

Adjust the cable machine so that the ankle straps are at the lowest setting. Lie face down on the bench with your legs extended and the ankle straps attached to your feet. Hold onto the handles of the bench for stability. Bend your knees and curl your legs towards your glutes, squeezing your hamstrings. Pause for a moment at the top of the movement, then slowly lower your legs back to the starting position. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

conditionNotes.note: Keep load symmetrical and stop at positions that create sharp pain or twisting.
Use pain-free range of motion, slower tempo, and lighter progressions when joints feel irritated.
Weight-bearing resistance exercises are beneficial for bone density when load is appropriate.
Maintain neutral spine and avoid positions that aggravate the lower back.
Maintain consistent breathing throughout. Avoid breath-holding.
Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor blood sugar before, during, and after sessions. Have a fast-acting carb available.
Single-leg or balance-intensive variations increase fall risk. Use support or a seated alternative.
Focus on muscle co-contraction and joint stability. Avoid locking out joints passively.
Functional patterns are generally safe. Prioritize pelvic floor awareness and avoid Valsalva.
Start light with higher reps and shorter ROM. Progress load gradually as pain-free capacity increases.
